cards.back: Every 20 min, look ~20 ft (6 m) away for 20 seconds to ease eye strain.

cards.front: How often to move when sitting?
cards.back: Stand/walk a couple minutes every 30-60 min.
Blue light suppresses melatonin and content keeps you alert, delaying sleep.
Pale-yellow urine means hydrated; dark means drink more.
It keeps you full, preserves muscle, and steadies energy.
Muscle is lost after ~30; resistance keeps strength, bone, metabolism.

cards.front: Why cut caffeine in the afternoon?
cards.back: Half-life ~5-6h, so it can still disrupt night sleep.
A consistent sleep/wake time sets your body clock.

cards.front: Why is a daily walk underrated?
cards.back: Low-impact movement lifts mood, blood sugar, and heart health.
Feeds gut bacteria, steadies blood sugar, keeps you full.
Bright light early anchors your circadian rhythm and improves night sleep.
Slow exhales (e.g. box breathing 4-4-4-4) shift you to "rest" mode.
Screen at eye level, feet flat, neutral spine — reduces neck/back strain.
It spikes then crashes blood sugar and energy; whole foods are steadier.
It helps you fall asleep but fragments deep sleep, so you wake unrested.
Keep it 10-20 min and not too late, so it refreshes without grogginess.
It raises blood flow and loosens muscles, cutting injury risk.
Washing hands with soap for ~20 seconds — simple and very effective.
You also lose electrolytes (sodium); replace them, not just water.
No — they grow during rest. Rest days are part of training, not a break from it.
Fullness signals lag ~20 min; slowing down helps you stop before overeating.
Stretch hip flexors and chest/neck — sitting tightens them and rounds posture.
Daily sunscreen — UV is the biggest preventable cause of skin aging.

cards.front: Why does weight-bearing exercise help bones?
cards.back: Stress signals bones to stay dense, lowering fracture risk with age.
